slides.title: Sets: No Duplicates Allowed
slides.body: A **set** is a collection that keeps only **unique** items — add the same thing twice and Python quietly keeps just one copy. Think of it as a guest list that automatically removes repeated names.
slides.tip: Sets are unordered, so Python won't remember what order you added things in.

slides.title: Creating a Set
slides.body: Use curly braces `{}` with comma-separated values. Python **automatically drops duplicates** for you — no extra code needed.
slides.tip: Empty `{}` creates a **dictionary**, not a set — use `set()` for an empty set.

slides.title: Fast Membership Checks
slides.body: Use `in` to test whether an item is in a set. Sets are **much faster** than lists for this — Python jumps straight to the answer instead of scanning every item.

slides.title: Combine Sets Like a Venn Diagram
slides.body: Four operators let you merge and compare sets: `|` union, `&` intersection, `-` difference, `^` symmetric difference.
slides.tip: `&` gives items in **both** sets; `-` gives items in the left set **only**.

slides.title: The Classic Deduplication Recipe
slides.body: Convert a list to a `set()` to strip duplicates instantly, then back to a sorted list when you need order. This three-step pattern shows up everywhere.
slides.tip: list → set → sorted list is one of Python's most useful one-liners.
